text:
Centre for Neurology Services

Neurosciences Division

Clinic Date: 08 November 2024

Dr Fiona
North Thames Community Health Centre
124 High Road, London N22 6EZ

Dear Dr Fiona,

Re: Susan Miller, DOB: 14-05-1986, Hospital No: P948572, NHS No: 7623549812
Address: 12 Baker Street, London, NW1 6XE

Diagnosis/Background

I reviewed Susan Miller today in clinic following reports of increasing difficulties in day-to-day functioning in the context of greater social pressure at work and higher alcohol exposure over recent months. She describes more frequent late nights, irregular meals, and occasional missed doses related to social events. There is no clear precipitating medical illness. Family remain supportive but have also noticed escalating stress and fatigue.

Seizure history and current frequency

Over the last year, seizure control has been relatively stable; she suffers generalised tonic-clonic seizures twice monthly, with a maximum seizure-free span of five weeks. focal clonic occur 4 per day. She also has drop attacks clustering every month. No seizures have been noted since her previous review. While the above summary reflects her baseline pattern over the past year, she and her partner report that the recent increase in social pressure and alcohol has made day-to-day management harder, with more near-miss episodes (brief spells of disorientation and startle) and reduced confidence outdoors. This pattern suggests a deterioration in overall control and safety, despite no documented seizures since the last review appointment.

Medication

She remains on lamotrigine dispersible 25 mg twice daily. She confirms full adherence over the last month after previously missing occasional evening doses when out socially. No adverse effects reported beyond mild nausea if taken on an empty stomach. We discussed her prior understanding of dosing; she believed an increase had been considered but was never actioned. I will recheck levels and liaise with the wider team for further planning.

Lamotrigine blood levels

No recent level available. A trough level has been requested (pre-morning dose) with a target sampling within the next week.

Examination/Assessment

Neurological examination today was non-focal. Cognition intact to conversation, but she appeared fatigued. Weight stable. No injuries since the last clinic, though she reports two near-falls during crowded commuting. Given the rising psychosocial stressors and increased alcohol exposure, her overall risk profile has worsened.

Plan

- Arrange urgent lamotrigine level and routine safety bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs) through your practice.
- I will contact you once levels are available to consider titration of lamotrigine if appropriate, in line with previous discussions.
- Patient-reported measures: she will keep a seizure and trigger diary, including alcohol intake, sleep pattern, and missed doses, to review at the next contact.
- Safety: reinforced harm-reduction strategies around alcohol and sleep regularity; she intends to limit intake and avoid late nights.
- Work and social stress: she will explore temporary adjustments with her employer to reduce late-shift exposure; she feels this is feasible and will report back.
- Follow-up: telephone review in 4 weeks to reassess control, diary data, and blood results; earlier if events escalate.

Yours sincerely,

She also has drop attacks clustering every month. No seizures have been noted since her previous review

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies mentioned: (1) "generalised tonic-clonic seizures twice monthly" → 2 per month. (2) "focal clonic occur 4 per day" → 4 per day. (3) "drop attacks clustering every month" → cluster info without per-cluster count, but frequency of clusters is monthly. Rule: choose the highest frequency across types. 4 per day is higher than 2 per month and monthly clusters. Although it states "No seizures have been noted since her previous review," duration since last review is not specified and is less than the 6‑month seizure‑free criterion; baseline frequency applies. Therefore, final label is 4 per day.
reference: Over the last year, seizure control has been relatively stable; she suffers generalised tonic-clonic seizures twice monthly, with a maximum seizure-free span of five weeks. focal clonic occur 4 per day. She also has drop attacks clustering every month. No seizures have been noted since her previous review
